steps.ordered: CT Abdomen (CT ABD & PELVIS WITH CONTRAST)

ensemble.other_hypothesis: bowel obstruction, mesenteric ischemia, colitis (infectious or ischemic), ovarian/gynecologic pathology, urologic source (ureteral stone, pyelonephritis)
ensemble.information_gap: The anatomic source and nature of the lower abdominal pathology driving the marked leukocytosis with bandemia cannot be determined from history, exam, and labs alone — the physician needs cross-sectional imaging to identify the specific organ involved and any complications (perforation, abscess, ischemia).
ensemble.expected_finding: Pericolonic fat stranding with wall thickening consistent with diverticulitis (given lower abdominal pain R>L in an elderly female), or alternatively an appendiceal abnormality, colitis, bowel obstruction, or pelvic/gynecologic mass
ensemble.appropriateness_reason: The combination of marked leukocytosis (WBC 23.6 K/uL with 12% bands and ANC 21.0 K/uL), lower abdominal tenderness, altered mental status, and a chest X-ray that excludes a pulmonary source creates a clear indication for CT abdomen/pelvis with contrast to identify the intra-abdominal source of sepsis in a patient who cannot reliably report symptoms due to dementia.
grounding: ABD: Soft, mildly distended, tender to palpation in lower abdomen R>L, no rebound/rigidity/guarding
History: dementia, DM, HTN, hypothyroidism, no prior abdominal operations
Chest X-ray: no focal consolidation, effusion, or edema
ALT: 12.0 IU/L, AST: 22.0 IU/L, Bilirubin Total: 0.5 mg/dL — hepatobiliary labs normal
ensemble.reasoning: This elderly woman with dementia presents with acute confusion, lower abdominal pain, and a striking leukocytosis with bandemia (WBC 23.6, 12% bands) pointing to a significant infectious or inflammatory intra-abdominal process; the chest X-ray has already excluded a pulmonary source, hepatobiliary labs and lipase are normal ruling out hepatobiliary and pancreatic causes, and the physical exam localizes tenderness to the lower abdomen R>L but cannot distinguish diverticulitis, appendicitis, colitis, mesenteric ischemia, or a gynecologic/urologic source in a patient who cannot reliably communicate. CT abdomen and pelvis with contrast is the single most efficient next step to anatomically localize the source, characterize severity, and identify any surgical emergency.

Chest X-ray: no focal consolidation, effusion, or edema

vindication.actual_finding: The CT demonstrated a dilated appendix measuring 9 mm (up from 5 mm on prior) with surrounding inflammation and hyperemia in the right lower quadrant, consistent with acute appendicitis, while the sigmoid diverticulosis showed no wall thickening or fat stranding.
vindication.relation_to_expectation: The physician's differential included an appendiceal abnormality as one of the expected findings, and the scan confirmed acute appendicitis rather than the leading suspicion of diverticulitis, still falling within the anticipated range of diagnoses.
